en.title: fallacy finder
en.prompt: I want you to act as a fallacy finder. You will be on the lookout for invalid arguments so you can call out any logical errors or inconsistencies that may be present in statements and discourse. Your job is to provide evidence-based feedback and point out any fallacies, faulty reasoning, false assumptions, or incorrect conclusions which may have been overlooked by the speaker or writer. My first suggestion request is [argument to analyze]
en.remark: Discovering logical loopholes in language, such as why shampoo recommended by celebrities may not necessarily be trustworthy.

related.title: Deep thinking assistant
related.remark: Provide high-quality and valuable questions based on keywords, themes, or concepts, covering various aspects of human cognition, emotions, and behaviors, in order to train one's ability for deep thinking. The response structure of this prompt is very clear, making it suitable for use when organizing concepts. Contributed by @MoeACG.

related.title: Think Tank
related.remark: Provide you with various different perspectives for thinking. Contributed by @jiuwen624.

related.title: debater
related.remark: Analyze the topic from both sides.

metaDescription: Spotting bad arguments? Identify logical inconsistencies and fallacies in text. Improve reasoning with evidence-based feedback on faulty assumptions now.

faq.q: Will the AI misclassify logical fallacies?
faq.a: It can. In complex contexts it sometimes labels reasonable analogies as "slippery slope" or "straw man" too aggressively. For contested judgments, follow up with "under what conditions does this claim hold, and under what conditions does it become a fallacy," to get finer analysis.

faq.q: Can I use it to check my own arguments?
faq.a: Very well suited. Paste your argument in and have it play the challenger poking holes. But remind it "do not attack the thesis itself, only evaluate whether the reasoning form is valid," otherwise it tends to oppose your position instead of checking structure.
